1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}

116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}

131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
300	
301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
306	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) the counit of the sheafification
307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,
310	  invertible because the right adjoint forgetful functor to presheaves of modules
311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}
313	
314	\begin{definition}\leanok
315	[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
316	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso}
318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
319	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
320	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical left-unitor
321	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
322	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
323	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of
324	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} and composing with the counit
325	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}. It is the base case
326	  (\(m = 0\)) of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{definition}\leanok
330	[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor
336	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X
337	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
338	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor
339	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) composed with the counit
340	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}.
341	\end{definition}
342	
343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
349	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
350	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
351	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
352	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
353	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
354	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
355	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
356	\end{definition}
357	
358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
365	[Localization criterion for module sheafification]
366	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_iff}
368	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
369	  For a morphism \(f : \mathcal{P} \to \mathcal{Q}\) of presheaves of
370	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the sheafification \((f)^{\#}\)
371	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules if
372	  and only if the underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves lies in the
373	  weak-equivalence class \(J.W\) of the opens topology \(J\) on \(X\) (i.e.\ is a
374	  local isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves).
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  Module sheafification (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) is the localization
380	  functor at the class \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying
381	  abelian-presheaf morphism lies in \(J.W\), i.e.\ the inverse image of \(J.W\) along
382	  the forgetful functor. The localization criterion for a reflective adjunction
383	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) then gives the stated
384	  equivalence.
385	\end{proof}
386	
387	\begin{lemma}\leanok
388	[The sheafification unit is an abelian local isomorphism]
389	  \label{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
391	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
392	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the underlying
393	  abelian-group presheaf morphism of the sheafification unit
394	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\)
395	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) is the abelian sheafification
396	  unit \(\mathcal{P} \to a(\mathcal{P})\); it therefore lies in the weak-equivalence
397	  class \(J.W\) of the opens topology.
398	\end{lemma}
399	
400	\begin{proof}
401	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
402	  The forgetful functor to abelian-group presheaves carries the module
403	  sheafification unit to the unit of the abelian associated-sheaf adjunction, which
404	  is a local isomorphism, hence a member of \(J.W\).
405	\end{proof}
406	
407	\begin{lemma}\leanok
408	[Sheafification inverts the localization unit]
409	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_map_unit}
411	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
412	  For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}\) the sheafification
413	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})^{\#}\) of the localization unit is an isomorphism of sheaves
414	  of modules. This is the un-whiskered (\(m = 0\)) special case of
415	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}.
416	\end{lemma}
417	
418	\begin{proof}
419	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit}
420	  Feed the abelian local-isomorphism statement
421	  \cref{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit} through the localization criterion
422	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff}.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\begin{definition}\leanok
426	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf]
427	  \label{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorDomainPresheaf}
429	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
430	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
431	  assignment \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\),
432	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the underlying restriction
433	  maps, is a functor \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is
434	  the codomain row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
435	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}); functoriality follows from the
436	  functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) (identity and composition laws
437	  by \(\otimes\)-induction).
438	\end{definition}
439	
440	\begin{definition}\leanok
441	[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
442	  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
444	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
445	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
446	  assignment
447	  \[
448	    U \;\longmapsto\;
449	      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
450	        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
451	  \]
452	  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
453	  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
454	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
455	  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
456	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
457	  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
458	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
459	  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
460	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
461	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
462	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, its codomain row being
463	  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
464	  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
465	  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
466	  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
467	  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
468	\end{definition}
469	
470	\begin{definition}\leanok
471	[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
472	  \label{def:relTensorActL}
473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
474	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
475	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
476	  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
477	  \[
478	    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
479	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
480	    \;\longrightarrow\;
481	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
482	  \]
483	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
484	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
485	  open \(U\), the additive map
486	  \[
487	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
488	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
489	    \longrightarrow
490	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
491	    \qquad
492	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
493	  \]
494	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
495	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
496	  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
497	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
498	  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
499	  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
500	  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
501	  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
502	  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
503	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; this is checked on
504	  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
505	\end{definition}
506	
507	\begin{definition}\leanok
508	[Right-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
509	  \label{def:relTensorActR}
510	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActR}
511	  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
512	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
513	  \emph{right-action} natural transformation
514	  \[
515	    a_R : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
516	      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
517	    \;\longrightarrow\;
518	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
519	  \]
520	  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
521	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
522	  open \(U\), the additive map
523	  \[
524	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
525	      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
526	    \longrightarrow
527	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
528	    \qquad
529	    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n),
530	  \]
531	  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
532	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{Q}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
533	  action map \(\mathrm{actRmap}\) of
534	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}, the mirror of the left-action
535	  transformation \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}); together \(a_L\) and \(a_R\)
536	  form the parallel pair whose coequalizer presents the relative tensor presheaf
537	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\). Naturality in \(U\) is the compatibility
538	  of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction of
539	  \(s \cdot n\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (n|_V)\), so \(a_R\) commutes with the
540	  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps. The verification
541	  reduces to the \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-semilinearity of the restriction maps of
542	  \(\mathcal{Q}\), checked on elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
543	\end{definition}
544	
545	\begin{definition}
546	
547	\leanok
548	[Projection natural transformation of the coequalizer]
549	  \label{def:relTensorProj}
550	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorProj}
551	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
552	  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
553	  \emph{projection} natural transformation
554	  \[
555	    \pi : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
556	      \;\longrightarrow\;
557	      \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
558	  \]
559	  from the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) to
560	  the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the presheaf-level monoidal tensor
561	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\) (Mathlib's
562	  \(\mathrm{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\)) is, on an open \(U\), the
563	  canonical quotient additive map
564	  \[
565	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
566	    \longrightarrow
567	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U),
568	    \qquad
569	    m \otimes n \longmapsto m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n,
570	  \]
571	  the objectwise projection \(\mathrm{projL}\) of
572	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}. It is the cofork of the
573	  relative-tensor coequalizer: the third leg, the cokernel of \(a_L - a_R\)
574	  (\cref{def:relTensorActL}, \cref{def:relTensorActR}). The apex is identified
575	  with the objectwise value of the presheaf monoidal product through
576	  \cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}.
577	
578	  \emph{Naturality.} The objectwise square asserts
579	  \[
580	    \pi_V \circ
581	      \bigl(\mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
582	        \mathrm{restr}_{\mathcal{Q}}\bigr)
583	    \;=\;
584	    \bigl((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\bigr) \circ \pi_U
585	    \qquad (f : V \to U),
586	  \]
587	  both composites sending \(m \otimes n\) to
588	  \(\bigl(m|_V\bigr) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} \bigl(n|_V\bigr)\). On elementary
589	  tensors the left-hand side reduces by functoriality of the
590	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of restriction maps followed by the defining formula of
591	  \(\mathrm{projL}\); the right-hand side reduces by \(\mathrm{projL}\) followed
592	  by the objectwise restriction formula for the presheaf monoidal product
593	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}, on elementary tensors:
594	  \((\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})(f)\,(m \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} n) =
595	  (m|_V) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(V)} (n|_V)\)). The two sides then agree by
596	  \(\otimes\)-induction.
597	
598	\end{definition}

600	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
601	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
602	  % NOTE: Lean decl name pending --- no `\lean{}` pin yet; add when the Lean
603	  % declaration for this whiskered-stalkwise-iso lemma lands.
604	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
605	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
606	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
607	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
608	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
609	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
610	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
611	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
612	\end{lemma}
613	
614	\begin{proof}
615	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
616	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
617	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
618	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
619	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
620	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
621	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
622	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
623	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
624	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
625	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
626	\end{proof}

628	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
629	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
630	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
631	  \mathlibok
632	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
633	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
634	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
635	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
636	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
637	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
638	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
639	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
640	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
641	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
642	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
643	\end{lemma}

645	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
646	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
648	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
649	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
650	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
651	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
652	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
653	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
654	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
655	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
656	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
657	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
658	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
659	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
660	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
661	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
662	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
663	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
664	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
665	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
666	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
667	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
668	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
669	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
670	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
671	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
672	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
673	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
674	  \[
675	    a_L,\, a_R :
676	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
677	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
678	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
679	  \]
680	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
681	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
682	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
683	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
684	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
685	\end{lemma}
686	
687	\begin{proof}
688	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
689	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
690	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
691	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
692	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
693	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
694	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
695	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
696	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
697	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
698	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
699	
700	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
701	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
702	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
703	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
704	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
705	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
706	
707	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
708	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
709	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
710	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
711	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
712	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
713	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
714	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
715	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
716	  colimit cofork.
717	
718	\end{proof}

720	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
721	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
722	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
723	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
724	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
725	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
726	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
727	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
728	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
729	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
730	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
731	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
732	  parallel pair
733	  \[
734	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
735	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
736	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
737	  \]
738	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
739	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
740	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
741	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
742	\end{lemma}
743	
744	\begin{proof}
745	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
746	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
747	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
748	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib,
749	    def:relTensorActL, def:relTensorActR, def:relTensorProj}
750	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
751	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
752	  three steps.
753	
754	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
755	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
756	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
757	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
758	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
759	  \[
760	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
761	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
762	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
763	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
764	  \]
765	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
766	
767	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families are precisely
768	  the natural transformations of presheaves on \(X\)
769	  \[
770	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
771	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
772	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
773	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
774	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
775	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
776	  \]
777	  built in the previous definitions: the parallel pair is the left- and
778	  right-action transformations \(a_L\) (\cref{def:relTensorActL}) and \(a_R\)
779	  (\cref{def:relTensorActR}) from the triple presheaf
780	  (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf
781	  (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}), and the cofork leg is the projection
782	  \(\pi\) (\cref{def:relTensorProj}) onto the apex. These are morphisms of
783	  functors \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\); naturality of
784	  each in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the restriction
785	  maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\) (the restriction of
786	  \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection),
787	  recorded in those definitions. Objectwise at each \(U\) the cofork
788	  \((a_L, a_R, \pi)_U\) is the colimit cofork of step 1. Colimits in a functor
789	  category are computed objectwise --- by
790	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
791	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
792	  coequalizers of step 1 promote to a coequalizer of the displayed parallel pair
793	  \(a_L, a_R\) with cofork \(\pi\) in
794	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\).
795	
796	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
797	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
798	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
799	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
800	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
801	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
802	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
803	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
804	  along this identification exhibits
805	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
806	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
807	\end{proof}

809	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
810	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
811	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
812	  \mathlibok
813	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
814	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
815	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
816	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
817	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
818	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
819	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
820	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
821	  category are computed objectwise.
822	\end{lemma}

844	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}

850	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,

853	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of

861	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of

863	  \[

867	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
868	  \]
869	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
870	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
871	\end{lemma}
872	
873	\begin{proof}
874	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
875	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
876	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
877	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
878	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
879	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
880	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
881	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
882	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
883	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
884	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
885	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
886	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
887	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
888	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
889	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
890	
891	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
892	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
893	  is the objectwise relative tensor
894	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
895	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
896	  maps
897	  \[
898	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
899	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
900	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
901	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
902	  \]
903	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
904	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
905	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
906	  pair
907	  \[
908	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
909	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
910	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
911	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
912	  \]
913	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
914	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
915	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
916	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
917	
918	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
919	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
920	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
921	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
922	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
923	  relating the rows,
924	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
925	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
926	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
927	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).
928	
929	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
930	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
931	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
932	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
933	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
934	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
935	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
936	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
937	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
938	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
939	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
940	  Under this identification the stalk of
941	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
942	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
943	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
944	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
945	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
946	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
947	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
948	
949	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
950	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
951	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
952	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
953	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
954	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
955	\end{proof}

1026	\begin{definition}
1027	
1028	\leanok
1029	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
1030	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
1031	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
1032	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor, def:gr_rankQuotient,
1033	    lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id, lem:gr_pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}
1034	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1035	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1036	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
1037	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1038	  % "represents the
1039	  %  contravariant functor
1040	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1041	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1042	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1043	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1044	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
1045	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1046	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
1047	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
1048	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
1049	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
1050	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
1051	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
1052	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
1053	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1054	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
1055	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
1056	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
1057	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
1058	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
1059	  \[
1060	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
1061	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
1062	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
1063	  \]
1064	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
1065	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
1066	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
1067	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
1068	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
1069	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
1070	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
1071	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
1072	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
1073	\end{definition}

1075	\section{The universal property}
1076	\label{sec:grquot_universal}

1078	\begin{theorem}
1079	
1080	\leanok
1081	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]

1086	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1087	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1088	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1089	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1090	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1091	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1092	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1093	  %  contravariant functor
1094	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1095	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1096	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1097	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1098	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.

1100	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1101	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1102	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1103	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1104	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1105	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1106	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1107	  \[
1108	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1109	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1110	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1111	    \qquad
1112	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1113	      \;\longmapsto\;
1114	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1115	  \]
1116	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1117	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1118	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1119	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1120	  universal family.
1121	\end{theorem}

1123	\begin{proof}
1124	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1125	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1126	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1127	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1128	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1129	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1130	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1131	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1132	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1133	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1134	
1135	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1136	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1137	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1138	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1139	  the composite
1140	  \[
1141	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1142	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1143	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1144	  \]
1145	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1146	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1147	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1148	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1149	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1150	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1151	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1152	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1153	
1154	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1155	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1156	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1157	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1158	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1159	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1160	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1161	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1162	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1163	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1164	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1165	
1166	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1167	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1168	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1169	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1170	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1171	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1172	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1173	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1174	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1175	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1176	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1177	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1178	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1179	
1180	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1181	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1182	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1183	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1184	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1185	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1186	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1187	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1188	
1189	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1190	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1191	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1192	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1193	\end{proof}

1029	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
1030	  \]
1031	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1032	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
1033	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
1034	\end{proof}

1036	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
1037	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1038	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
1039	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
1040	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
1041	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
1042	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
1043	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1044	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
1045	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
1046	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
1047	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
1048	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
1049	  %  (formulation omitted)."
1050	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
1051	  power isomorphisms).}
1052	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
1053	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1054	  \[
1055	    \mu_{m,m'} :
1056	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1057	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
1058	  \]
1059	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
1060	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
1061	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
1062	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1063	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
1064	\end{lemma}
1065	
1066	\begin{proof}
1067	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
1068	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
1069	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
1070	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1071	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1072	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
1073	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
1074	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
1075	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
1076	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
1077	
1078	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
1079	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
1080	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
1081	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
1082	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
1083	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
1084	  sheaf tensor products
1085	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
1086	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
1087	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
1088	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
1089	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
1090	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
1091	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
1092	  of the tensor product is never used.
1093	
1094	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
1095	  \(m = 0\),
1096	  \[
1097	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1098	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1099	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1100	  \]
1101	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
1102	  recursive definition
1103	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
1104	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
1105	  \[
1106	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1107	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
1108	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
1109	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1110	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
1111	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
1112	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
1113	  \]
1114	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
1115	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
1116	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
1117	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
1118	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
1119	  \[
1120	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
1121	      \mathcal{L}
1122	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
1123	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
1124	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
1125	  \]
1126	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
1127	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
1128	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
1129	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
1130	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
1131	
1132	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
1133	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
1134	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
1135	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
1136	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
1137	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
1138	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
1139	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
1140	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
1141	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
1142	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
1143	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
1144	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
1145	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
1146	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
1147	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
1148	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
1149	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
1150	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1151	\end{proof}

1153	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
1154	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
1155	
1156	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
1157	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
1158	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
1159	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
1160	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
1161	global sections.
1162	
1163	\begin{definition}\leanok
1164	[Section multiplication]
1165	  \label{def:sectionMul}
1166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
1167	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1168	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1169	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1170	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1171	  \[
1172	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1173	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1174	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1175	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1176	  \]
1177	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1178	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1179	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1180	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1181	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1182	  the sheafification unit
1183	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1184	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1185	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1186	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1187	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1188	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1189	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1190	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1191	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1192	\end{definition}
1193	
1194	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1195	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1196	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1197	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1198	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
1199	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
1200	  the two composites
1201	  \[
1202	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
1203	    \longrightarrow
1204	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
1205	    \cong
1206	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
1207	  \]
1208	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
1209	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
1210	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
1211	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
1212	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
1213	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
1214	\end{lemma}
1215	
1216	\begin{proof}
1217	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
1218	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1219	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
1220	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
1221	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
1222	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
1223	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
1224	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
1225	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
1226	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
1227	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
1228	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
1229	  section multiplications.
1230	\end{proof}

1232	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1233	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1234	
1235	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1236	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1237	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1238	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1239	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1240	
1241	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1242	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1243	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1244	  \mathlibok
1245	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1246	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1247	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1248	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1249	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1250	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1251	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1252	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1253	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1254	\end{lemma}
1255	
1256	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1257	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1258	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1259	  \mathlibok
1260	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1261	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1262	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1263	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1264	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1265	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1266	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1267	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1268	  extension of the graded action.
1269	\end{lemma}
1270	
1271	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1272	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1274	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1275	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
1276	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1277	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1278	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1279	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1281	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1282	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1283	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1284	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1285	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
1286	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
1287	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
1288	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
1289	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1290	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1291	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1292	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1293	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1294	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1295	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1296	  multiplication is the composite
1297	  \[
1298	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1299	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1300	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1301	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1302	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1303	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1304	  \]
1305	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1306	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1307	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1308	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1309	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1310	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1311	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1312	\end{lemma}

1314	\begin{proof}
1315	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
1316	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1317	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
1318	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
1319	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
1320	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
1321	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
1322	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
1323	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
1324	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
1325	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1326	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
1327	  commutative-semiring structure.

1330	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]

1352	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)

1354	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded

1369	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
1370	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
1371	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
1372	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
1373	\end{lemma}
1374	
1375	\begin{proof}
1376	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
1377	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1378	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
1379	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
1380	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
1381	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
1382	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
1383	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
1384	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
1385	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
1386	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
1387	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
1388	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
1389	\end{proof}
